identified_error: The paper’s core error was selection on statistical significance: labs only advanced effects from the exploratory/pilot phase when they believed they had found a new effect that was statistically distinguishable from zero. The subsequent ‘self-confirmatory’ and replication studies were then interpreted as evidence that rigorous practices yield high replicability. But because candidate findings were screened in noisy pilot data and chosen for having positive results, the set of 16 effects was enriched for upwardly biased estimates and chance-positive findings. This makes the reported replication rate and effect-size stability a consequence of the selection procedure, not valid evidence about a general ‘replicability ceiling’ under best practices.
why_fatal: This invalidates the main conclusion because the study does not estimate replicability for discoveries generated under rigorous methods in any unbiased sense. It estimates replicability of a handpicked subset of exploratory findings that survived an initial significance filter. Once you condition on an initial positive pilot result, later large-sample studies are no longer independent tests of whether rigorous practices produce highly replicable discoveries overall. The design confounds methodological rigor with winner’s-curse/selective advancement: many null or weak pilot ideas were never entered, while only apparent ‘discoveries’ were. As a result, the observed 81–90% confirmation/replication success cannot support the paper’s broad claim that high replicability is achievable or likely under best practices, because the denominator of all attempted discoveries was excluded. The paper therefore overstates what its data can show.
correct_approach: The correct approach would have been to define the sampling frame prospectively and include all eligible discovery attempts, not only those that looked significant in pilot work. For example: preregister a rule that every idea meeting design criteria within a period is carried forward to confirmation/replication, or use a random sample of exploratory findings regardless of pilot significance. Analyses should then estimate the full pipeline success rate from exploration to confirmation to independent replication, accounting for the selection process. At minimum, any claims should have been limited to the conditional statement that among findings selected after promising pilot evidence, large preregistered follow-ups can replicate at a high rate. Stronger claims about replicability under rigorous practice require modeling or eliminating the initial selection bias.
additional_errors: The manuscript compares replication power to effect sizes estimated from the self-confirmatory studies as if those were fixed targets, without adequately addressing uncertainty and prior selection.
The 16 findings were not a representative sample of social-behavioral research topics or methods, limiting generalization even aside from the selection problem.
Allowing labs to decide which pilots were ‘promising’ introduces subjective researcher degrees of freedom in nomination of effects.

Claims about absence of decline in effect sizes are weakened because the set of studied effects had already passed an initial positive screen and may exclude effects that would have failed or attenuated earlier.
Different exclusion practices and clarification exchanges across labs may have added uncontrolled heterogeneity, though this is secondary to the fatal selection issue.
